page: Amazon Web Services m3.large is a general-purpose instance belonging to the m3 server family. It is powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. The server provides 2 virtual CPUs mapped to a single physical core with hyper-threading enabled, running at a clock speed of 2.5 GHz. It features 8.0 GB of system memory, translating to 3.75 GB of RAM per core, and includes 32 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Network connectivity is rated at a baseline bandwidth of 1 Gbps. The virtualization layer is managed via the Xen hypervisor.

In benchmark evaluations, the m3.large demonstrates entry-level performance characteristics. Its single-core CPU performance under stress-ng is categorized as weak, falling into the bottom 25%, while its multi-core CPU performance is poor, placing in the bottom 10%. Memory bandwidth tests show read-only small block cached speeds of 92.83 GB/sec and uncached large block write speeds of 12.02 GB/sec, both of which rank in the bottom 10%. Similarly, Geekbench scores reflect legacy hardware capabilities, with a single-core score of 693.0 and a multi-core score of 802.0.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m3.large represents an older generation hardware profile. While it offers dedicated CPU resources and bundled local NVMe SSD storage, its resource density and performance-per-resource ratios are low compared to modern cloud instances. This makes it a legacy option where compatibility or specific older generation configurations are required, rather than a primary choice for resource-intensive modern applications.

Appropriate workloads for the m3.large are limited to low-demand general-purpose tasks. These include basic web serving, lightweight background processes, small-scale development or testing environments, and simple utility servers that do not require high computational throughput or massive memory bandwidth.
description: Amazon Web Services m3.large is a legacy general-purpose instance powered by a 2.5 GHz Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor. Utilizing the x86_64 architecture, it features 2 vCPUs on a single physical core, 8.0 GB of RAM, and 32 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Running on the Xen hypervisor, it delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Benchmark data indicates entry-level performance, with stress-ng single-core results in the bottom 25% and multi-core results in the bottom 10%. Memory bandwidth and Geekbench scores also place the instance in the lower performance tiers. Given its older hardware profile, the m3.large is best suited for low-intensity workloads, such as basic web serving, lightweight utility tasks, or legacy development environments that require dedicated but modest compute resources without high throughput demands.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m3.large is a general-purpose instance featuring 2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM, and 32 GB NVMe SSD storage, delivering entry-level performance for legacy or low-demand workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m3.large is a general-purpose instance with 2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM, and 32 GB NVMe SSD storage, running on the Xen hypervisor.
tagline: An entry-level general-purpose instance with dedicated legacy compute resources and local NVMe SSD storage for basic utility workloads.

Bundled 32 GB local NVMe SSD storage
1 Gbps baseline network bandwidth on Xen hypervisor
Entry-level performance profile suited for legacy applications
Best for basic web serving and lightweight utility tasks
